comment_content:
One thing I noticed about Joe. Hes different from other men, but also the same.

He's different because he's a deep thinker. He loves books and loves to read. He's a hopeless romantic and idealizes love. He's always looked for love in all the wrong places. He's got depth to him, but almost too much depth. He gets inside his head way too much. A lot of guys in comparison to him are just very basic. They lack the depth he has which is why so many women are drawn to him. He's interesting and seems like a nice guy.

How he's similar to other guys: At the end of the day, he's thinking about his own needs and wants and not truly looking for a real partner. He's drawn to broken women because he's broken and wants to be the hero in all of their lives. But he's also drawn to strong, independent women because he looks up to them in a way and wants to be like them. He definitely knows how to turn on the charm & he silently studies all his love interests aka victims and turns himself into what they like. He's quick to lose interest in every relationship as soon as things become to normalized. He needs the drama & spark to keep things alive opposite of his victims. It's the high that he's constantly chasing after with these women, the fantasy, but as soon as reality hits he doesn't want it anymore and he's off to his next victim. He's truly emotionally unavailable at the core, and the more he can't have someone he's determined to win them over.

reply_content: ​@destiny2222 I appreciate your opinion but I also respectfully disagree. For me, shows like "You" shine a light on dangerous behaviours that you would otherwise be unaware of. By way of a very simple example, in Season 1 Joe was able to track Beck down from her social media - I think that served as an important reminder to all that your social media can be used by dangerous people. I think that this was a message a lot of people needed to see / hear. On a deeper level, "You" delved into the psychology of an abusive, love-bombing narcissist and how we, as a society, fail to condemn behaviours and actions committed by a certain demographic in society because they are said demographic. I don't think his behaviours are romanticised - they are shown for what they are time and time again by non-Joe characters

comment_content: “which he builds himself. every season.” 😆
reply_content:
I don’t think You is meant to glamorize or justify Joe’s actions at all, quite the opposite. It’s doesnt patronize behavior like his, even Penn hataes him lol; it holds up a mirror to it. The brilliance of the show is how it lures us into seeing the world through Joe’s distorted lens — and then forces us to confront how easily manipulation and control can be disguised as love.

It’s uncomfortable, yes, but that discomfort is intentional. It’s a tool for reflection, not validation. The fact that people sometimes find themselves empathizing with or rooting for Joe is part of the critique, it exposes how media and society can blur the lines between passion and possession, charm and control.
